title: Reading and Use of English - Part 8
description: Read the four texts about journalism. For questions 47 – 56, choose from the experts (A – D).

content.title: The Death and Rebirth of the News

texts.A:
### The Traditional Editor (David)
David misses the days when editors were the 'curators' of reality. [47]'Now, everyone is their own editor, and the result is a fragmented truth,' he says.[/47] [51]He argues that the collapse of local newspapers has created 'news deserts' where corruption goes unchecked.[/51] [54]He highlights that the speed of the 24-hour news cycle has made 'being first' more important than 'being right'.[/54] [56]For him, the only solution is a return to a subscription model where readers pay for quality rather than being the product of advertisers.[/56]
texts.B:
### The Citizen Journalist (Aisha)
Aisha believes that social media has 'democratized' the news. [48]She points out that a teenager with a smartphone can now expose an injustice that a major TV network would ignore.[/48] [52]She acknowledges the problem of fake news but argues that the crowd is often better at 'fact-checking' in real-time than a single editor.[/52] [50]She mentions that the biggest challenge is 'echo chambers', where people only consume news that confirms their existing biases.[/50] [53]However, she highlights that the direct connection between reporters and their audience creates a new kind of trust.[/53]
texts.C:
### The Data Analyst (Sam)
Sam focuses on how algorithms decide what we see. [49]He warns that the 'outage' of emotional news—stories that make us angry or sad—is prioritized because it generates more clicks.[/49] [55]He highlights that investigative journalism is being replaced by 'churnalism'—reprocessing press releases to save money.[/55] [52]He suggests that the future of news will be personalized by AI, but warns this could lead to a total loss of a 'shared reality'.[/52] [51]He mentions that many people now get their news from memes rather than articles.[/51]
texts.D:
### The Investigative Reporter (Clara)
Clara works for a non-profit newsroom funded by donations. [48]She points out that this model allows her to spend six months on a single story without worrying about daily traffic.[/48] [53]She mentions that 'slow journalism' is the only way to tackle complex global issues like tax havens.[/53] [50]She acknowledges that while trust in media is at an all-time low, people are still willing to support 'mission-driven' reporting.[/50] [54]She warns that the biggest threat to her work is not a lack of money, but legal harassment from powerful figures trying to silence her.[/54] [56]For Clara, the value of a story is measured by its impact, not its views.[/56]

questions.question: express concern that the lack of central oversight has led to a loss of consensus on facts?
questions.explanation: David says everyone is their own editor and the result is a 'fragmented truth'.

questions.question: mention a financial model that prioritizes in-depth research over immediate popularity?
questions.explanation: Clara works for a non-profit that allows her to spend six months on a story regardless of traffic.

questions.question: state that news platforms exploit human emotions to maximize engagement?
questions.explanation: Sam warns that news that makes us angry/sad is prioritized for clicks.

questions.question: acknowledge that people's tendency to seek out reinforcing information is a major problem?
questions.explanation: Aisha mentions the challenge of 'echo chambers' confirming existing biases.

questions.question: note a shift in the medium through which the public consumes information?
questions.explanation: Sam mentions people getting news from memes; David (A) mentions the loss of local newspapers.

questions.question: argue that a collective of individuals can be more effective at verifying information than a professional?
questions.explanation: Aisha argues that the 'crowd' is often better at real-time fact-checking.

questions.question: suggest that certain topics require a much slower pace of reporting to be understood?
questions.explanation: Clara mentions 'slow journalism' is the only way to tackle complex global issues.

questions.question: identify a threat to journalism that comes from outside the industry's financial problems?
questions.explanation: Clara identifies 'legal harassment from powerful figures' as her biggest threat.

questions.question: complain that original reporting is being traded for the easy recycling of existing information?
questions.explanation: Sam highlights 'churnalism'—reprocessing press releases to save money.

questions.question: claim that the true worth of a piece of news is not found in its statistical reach?
questions.explanation: Clara says value is measured by impact, not views. (David A also mentions paying for quality).
